field-notes:
1.1 Number Systems
  • Binary (Base-2): 0s and 1s. Used by logic gates/switches.
  • Hexadecimal (Base-16): Compact representation. Used for MAC addresses, HTML colors, Error codes.
  • Binary Arithmetic:
    • Overflow: Result exceeds allocated bits (e.g., >255 in 8-bit).
    • Logical Shift: Left shift = Multiply by 2. Right shift = Divide by 2. Bits lost at ends.
    • Two's Complement: Represents negative numbers. Flip bits and add 1. Leftmost bit is sign bit (1=negative).

1.2 Media Representation
  • Text: ASCII (7-bit, English) vs Unicode (16/32-bit, Global languages/Emojis).
  • Sound: Analogue converted to Digital via sampling.
    • Higher Sample Rate (Hz) & Resolution (bits) = Better quality but larger file.
  • Images: Bitmap consists of pixels.
    • Colour Depth: Bits per pixel (e.g., 8-bit = 256 colours).
    • Resolution: W x H. Higher res/depth = Larger file.

1.3 Storage & Compression
  • Units: Bit, Nibble, Byte, KiB ($2^{10}$), MiB, GiB, TiB, PiB, EiB.
  • Compression:
    • Lossy: Permanently deletes data (JPG, MP3). Reduces quality significantly.
    • Lossless: Compresses without data loss (RLE, ZIP, PNG). Uses algorithms like Run Length Encoding.
field-summary: Computers use binary for processing. We use Hex for human readability. Media quality is a trade-off between fidelity (resolution/sampling) and file size. Compression (Lossy/Lossless) manages this size.
field-keywords: Binary, Hexadecimal, Overflow, Logical Shift, Two's Complement, ASCII, Unicode, Sample Rate, Resolution, Colour Depth, Lossy, Lossless

checklist-data.text: 1.1.3: Uses of Hexadecimal (MAC, Color codes, Errors)

field-notes:
2.1 Transmission Basics
  • Packets: Header (IPs, ID), Payload (Data), Trailer (EOF, CRC).
  • Packet Switching: Packets take different routes via routers, arrive out of order, reassembled at destination.
  • Methods:
    • Serial: 1 bit/wire. Reliable over distance (USB). Slower but no skew.
    • Parallel: Multiple bits/wires. Fast, internal use (IC). Prone to interference/skew.
  • Direction: Simplex (1-way), Half-Duplex (Both, turn-taking), Full-Duplex (Simultaneous).
  • USB: Universal Serial Bus. Standard, 1-way insertion, powers devices, auto-driver detection.

2.2 Error Detection
  • Parity Check: Adds bit to make 1s odd/even. Checks byte (horizontal) and block (vertical) to find errors.
  • Checksum: Algorithm calculates value from data; receiver recalculates and compares.
  • Echo Check: Data sent back to sender to compare.
  • ARQ: Automatic Repeat Query. Uses Acknowledgement and Timeout.
  • Check Digit: Final digit calculated from others (Barcode/ISBN) to spot entry errors.

2.3 Encryption
  • Symmetric: Same key for encrypt/decrypt. Key distribution risk.
  • Asymmetric: Public key (encrypt) and Private key (decrypt). More secure.
field-summary: Data moves in packets. Serial transmission is standard for peripherals (USB). Error detection relies on math (checksums/parity) or protocols (ARQ). Encryption secures data using keys.
field-keywords: Packet Switching, Serial, Parallel, Simplex, Duplex, USB, Parity, Checksum, Echo Check, ARQ, Check Digit, Symmetric, Asymmetric

field-cues:
Von Neumann Components?


F-E Cycle Steps?



Primary vs Secondary?


MAC vs IP?
field-notes:
3.1 CPU Architecture (Von Neumann)
  • Components: ALU (Math/Logic), CU (Decodes/Controls), Registers (Fast memory).
  • Registers: PC (Next Address), MAR (Current Address), MDR (Data), CIR (Current Instruction), ACC (ALU result).
  • Buses: Address (Unidirectional), Data (Bi), Control (Bi/Uni).
  • F-E Cycle: Fetch (PC→MAR→RAM→MDR→CIR, PC+1), Decode (CU reads CIR), Execute (ALU/Jump).
  • Performance: Cores (Parallel processing), Cache (Fast access to freq data), Clock (Hz).
  • Embedded Systems: Dedicated function (washing machine). Reliable, fixed hardware.

3.2 Input/Output
  • Input: 2D/3D Scanners, Barcode/QR readers, Cameras, Mics, Capacitive/Resistive Screens.
  • Sensors: Light, Temp, Magnetic, pH, Moisture, Humidity, Gas, Pressure, Acoustic, Infra-red.
  • Output: Actuators (Motors), DLP/LCD Projectors, Inkjet/Laser/3D Printers, LED/LCD Screens.

3.3 Storage
  • Primary: RAM (Volatile, R/W, OS/Apps in use), ROM (Non-volatile, Boot/BIOS).
  • Secondary: Magnetic (HDD - moving parts), Optical (CD/DVD - lasers/pits), SSD (Flash - fast/durable).
  • Virtual Memory: Uses HDD as RAM extension when RAM full. Slower (Thrashing).

3.4 Network HW: NIC (Interface), MAC (Hardware ID, Hex), IP (Network ID, IPv4/v6), Router (Connects networks).
field-summary: The CPU (Von Neumann) processes data fetched from RAM. Performance depends on cores/cache. Input comes from sensors/devices; Output goes to screens/actuators. Data stored permanently on Secondary storage.
field-keywords: Von Neumann, ALU, CU, MAR, MDR, PC, ACC, F-E Cycle, Cores, Cache, Embedded System, Sensors, Actuators, RAM/ROM, SSD, MAC, IP

field-cues:
System vs App SW?

OS Key Functions?



Interrupt Process?


Compiler vs Interpreter?
field-notes:
4.1 Software Types
  • System Software: Runs the hardware (OS, Drivers, Utilities).
  • Application Software: User tasks (Word, Excel, Games).
  • Operating System (OS):
    • Manages: Interface (CLI/GUI), Memory, Multitasking, Peripherals (Drivers), Files, User Accounts, Security.
  • Firmware: Bootloader on ROM/EEPROM. Starts the OS.
  • Interrupts: Signals to CPU to stop current task.
    • Sources: HW (Printer error, Key press), SW (Divide by zero).
    • Action: Save registers → Run ISR (Service Routine) → Restore registers → Resume.

4.2 Languages & Translators
  • High-Level: Portable, English-like (Python). Needs translation.
  • Low-Level: Assembly (Mnemonics, HW specific), Machine Code (Binary).
  • Translators:
    • Assembler: Assembly → Machine Code.
    • Compiler: Whole code → EXE. Error list at end. Fast execution. Protects source.
    • Interpreter: Line-by-line. Stops at error. Good for debugging. Slower run.
  • IDE Features: Editor (Syntax highlight), Runtime env, Debugger (Step/Breakpoints), Auto-complete.
field-summary: Software is layered: Apps on OS on Firmware on Hardware. The OS manages resources and Interrupts. We code in High-Level (via IDEs) which is translated (Compiled/Interpreted) into Machine Code.
field-keywords: System SW, App SW, OS, Interrupt, ISR, Firmware, High-level, Low-level, Assembly, Compiler, Interpreter, IDE

field-notes:
5.1 Internet & WWW
  • Internet: Infrastructure (Networks). WWW: Service (Pages/HTML).
  • URL: Protocol (https) :// Domain (google.com) / Path.
  • DNS: Translates Domain Name → IP Address. browser asks DNS server; DNS returns IP.
  • HTTP vs HTTPS: 'S' = Secure (Encrypted).
  • Cookies: Session (Ram, temporary) vs Persistent (HDD, login/prefs).

5.2 Digital Currency
  • Blockchain: Decentralized, immutable digital ledger. Tracks transactions securely.

5.3 Cyber Security
  • Threats:
    • Malware: Virus (replicating), Worm (network spreading), Trojan (hidden), Spyware (keylogger), Ransomware (locks data).
    • Attacks: Phishing (Fake email), Pharming (DNS redirect), DDoS (Server flood), Brute-Force (Guessing passwords), Social Engineering.
  • Solutions:
    • Firewall: Filters traffic (In/Out).
    • SSL/TLS: Encrypts connection (Padlock icon).
    • Proxy Server: Hides IP, filters content, caches pages.
    • Auth: Biometrics, 2FA, Strong passwords.
field-summary: The Internet uses DNS to find sites and HTTPS to secure them. Blockchain secures currency. Security is a war between Threats (Malware/Phishing) and Solutions (Firewalls/Encryption).
field-keywords: URL, DNS, HTTP/S, Blockchain, Ledger, Malware, Phishing, DOS, Firewall, SSL, Proxy, Biometrics

field-notes:
6.1 Automated Systems
  • The Loop: Sensor (Input) → ADC (Analogue to Digital) → Microprocessor (Compares to Preset) → DAC (if needed) → Actuator (Output).
  • Advantages: Consistent, 24/7, Safer in danger zones. Disadvantages: Job loss, Initial cost.

6.2 Robotics
  • Characteristics: Mechanical structure, Electrical components (Sensors/Actuators), Programmable.
  • Uses: Industry (Assembly), Transport (Drones), Agriculture, Medicine (Surgery).

6.3 Artificial Intelligence (AI)
  • Definition: Simulating intelligent behaviour (Reasoning, Learning, Adapting).
  • Expert Systems: Knowledge Base (Facts) + Inference Engine (Logic) + Interface. Used for diagnosis (Medical/Car).
  • Machine Learning: System learns/adapts from data without explicit programming.
field-summary: Automation relies on the Sensor-Processor-Actuator loop. Robots combine this with mechanics. AI adds the layer of "learning" and "reasoning" to data.
field-keywords: Sensors, Microprocessor, Actuator, ADC/DAC, Loop, Robotics, AI, Expert Systems, Machine Learning

field-notes:
7.1 Lifecycle: Analysis (Decomposition/Abstraction) → Design → Coding → Testing.

7.2 Tools: Flowcharts (Shapes: Diamond=Decision, Rect=Process, Parallel=I/O) & Pseudocode.

7.3 Data Checking
  • Validation (Is it reasonable?): Range, Length, Type, Presence, Format, Check Digit.
  • Verification (Is it copied right?): Visual Check, Double Entry.

7.6 Test Data
  • Normal: Expected. Abnormal: Wrong type/invalid. Boundary: Edge cases. Extreme: Limits.

7.4 Standard Algorithms
  • Linear Search: Check each item in order. Slow.
  • Bubble Sort: Swap adjacent pairs. Multiple passes.
  • Others: Totalling (Sum=Sum+n), Counting (C=C+1), Finding Max/Min.
field-summary: Good code requires Design (Flowcharts) and Testing. We validate inputs to prevent crashes and verify them to prevent errors. Standard algorithms (Sort/Search) are building blocks.
field-keywords: Analysis, Design, Coding, Testing, Flowchart, Pseudocode, Validation, Verification, Trace Table, Linear Search, Bubble Sort, Test Data
